text: "Now Science will tell you....." this is one of the biggest mistakes regarding "Science", as a verb it's empirical as a noun & especially personified it doesn't have any empirical existence, it can't tell us anything, it's not a person, has no mouth & therefore can't talk, it's not even an "it" but more of a "they". "We" is also problematic, in that it's not specified. If paradigm theory is true & is itself a paradigm, what will replace it? (Clue; it isn't true, historically it's paradigms).

author: @johnwilson3668
text: Anyone associated with universities who go's against doctrine is vilified . I personally do not go along with  black holes .dark matter and dark energy as they envisage it . As an engineer I look at things from my prospective example Black holes .They say there is a black hole in the centre of the galaxy not as I see it .My concept is that the galaxy is spinning therefore there must be a void in the centre . We know that time is caused by Rotation and gravity ,so in the centre of the galaxy time does not exist .so if anything that falls into the centre it will fall apart and be expelled  in a stream of high energy particles .My explanation fits what  has been observed better than black holes which are supposed to suck everything in . Observations have shown matter streams being expelled from the centre of far away galaxies . If I put this idea forward in a university they would throw me out the door

author: @brixomatic
text:
Well, science may have its problem with ignorance or let's say it is really skeptical of unusual ideas; just think of the theory of relativity or quantum theory, which had a hard time to get accepted, but these theories finally were accepted, because they proved to be incredibly accurate in terms of their predictions. But these ideas didn't fall out of the sky either, it was rather the problems of the old theories, new insights and findings and the elimination of hypotheses that didn't work, that lead to these theories. It's not that Einstein woke up and said: "Heureka! I'm gonna do something completely different!", which seems to be common belief. For the theory of relativity, a lot of other people's work already pointed into this direction and if it hadn't been for Einstein, someone else would have done it inevitably, maybe some years later. My perception is, that ideas come naturally, when there is enough knowledge to base them on. Like inventing the telephone was a natural step from knowledge of electricity and using signals as a language meeting the demand to communicate over larger distances. Sadly a lot of people do think about new theories as new ideas that suddenly come across one's mind and revolutionize everything out of nothing. So there are people who just have some crazy idea of how the world would look like, surprisingly often without a strong scientific background, and they just run out, tell the world, bending everything possible theory beyond recognition to match the idea (often without in depth knowledge of these theories), ignoring everything that contradicts their idea and claim science is a fraud because scientists have a strong financial interest or [add your favorite conspiracy hypothesis here]. Who can take these people serious? And then there are those people who know they're a fraud, that actually use some pseudo-scientific language and made up claims of success and conspiracy to fool those who like to believe in the "little inventor that revolutionizes the world of science  in a flash of brilliance" and who believe that there is so much that science can't explain (often just not knowing existing explanations). How many "water powered" cars have we seen, how many "free energy devices" and "cold fusion" claims have been busted, how much "pyramid power" esoteric BS has been proven to exist solely for extracting money out of naive people selling books and getting paid for speaking on pseudo-science-forums  or just getting one's 15 minutes of fame?

You really have to admit, science may have its limits and its problems within the "community", but you will hopefully agree, that it is the best tool we've got and I can't imagine any better one, I might add.

 And of course science can't bother with every nerd and his new idea or new hypothesis - if it did, it would bring science to a grinding halt, minding too much about crazy stuff than about refining what we already know and taking this one step further until we hit a limit of knowledge, waiting for other things to come together until they finally lead the path for new ideas.

author: @nitelite78
text:
I would take issue with calling science a religion. Religion is not really defined as a "set" of beliefs but rather it has some supernatural element, usually a god or gods attached to it's definition.

If you define religion as a "set of beliefs" then you would have to include all sorts of other sets of beliefs under the religion definition including for example marxism, capitalist views, socialism.

Then if you do that you would just need to find a new word to describe world views that have supernatural claims at the core. So why bother? Just leave religion to mean world views with gods. It makes more sense to me.

Also I think saying science is based on a "lie" is a little unfair. Rather than the word lie I would phrase it something like "epistemological limitations".

Although I would that it is important that we face those limitations and the vast majority of people aren't aware of the circular nature of our reasoning at all.

Interesting video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Religion is an organized collection of beliefs, cultural systems, and world views that relate humanity to an order of existence. Many religions have narratives, symbols, and sacred histories that are intended to explain the meaning of life and/or to explain the origin of life or the Universe - not something capitilists try to do much of. However the existence of gods is not necessary to religion per se - for example Buddhism.Sikhism is very interesting in this respect as the deity central to that religion is Waheguru which is often translated as supreme being but equally as often translated as great teacher .
The idea that science is limited by it's understanding of underlying knowledge is not a view I have much sympathy with - I would rather say that what unites all these systems and gives them striking similarity is their claim that they hold, ultimately, the truth and that they are the only road to the truth. If you ponder this you will see science and religion have some striking similarities right down to the persecution of unbelievers - still - i appreciate your points and think they are sensible and worthy of thought - thank you for adding to the discussion

author: @JustinHallPlus
text: I always remind my friends that science doesn't contain proofs, only evidence.  Proofs only exist in pure mathematics, and pure mathematics is a mentally constructed tool that isn't really compatible with empirical evidence.  And that's all you can get from experimentation.
